summary: Jeff Atwood explores Michael Abrash's Pixomatic, a software-only 3D renderer that accomplishes the seemingly impossible task of making a CPU compete with dedicated GPU hardware. Testing it on his modern (for 2009) hardware, Atwood finds the renderer delivers playable framerates in Unreal Tournament 2004, achieving 30-60 fps at reasonable settings. However, he acknowledges this achievement is 'utterly pointless' from a practical standpoint, since even basic integrated GPUs vastly outperform it. Despite the lack of practical utility, Atwood celebrates the engineering achievement and expresses admiration for 'heroic coding' - pushing technology to its absolute limits even when the results have no commercial value.
key_points: Pixomatic achieved what many thought impossible: a pure software 3D renderer that could run modern games at playable framerates
On 2009 hardware, the software renderer delivered 30-60 fps in Unreal Tournament 2004 at 1024x768 with decent quality settings
The achievement is acknowledged as 'utterly pointless' since even cheap integrated GPUs vastly outperform software rendering
Intel's Larrabee represented an attempt to blur the line between CPU and GPU, potentially making such optimization work more relevant
There's inherent value in 'heroic coding' - pushing boundaries of what's possible even without practical application
money_quotes: Pixomatic was perhaps the greatest performance challenge I've ever encountered, certainly right up there with Quake.
Pretty amazing, right? It is! And utterly pointless.
My current video card renders Unreal Tournament 2004 at the highest possible resolution with every possible quality option set to maximum, at somewhere between 200 and 300 frames per second.
Despite the miraculously efficient assembly Abrash and Sartain created to make this possible at all, it's at best a carnival oddity.
I can't help admiring the engineering effort that went into building a viable 3D software renderer, something that seemed virtually impossible bordering on foolish.
I happen to like heroic coding.
key_insight: There's profound value in pushing technology to its absolute limits through heroic coding efforts, even when the practical utility of the achievement is essentially zero.
